nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving freshwater streams, ponds, lakes, rivers, and canals — typically nutrient-rich, shallow waters of the Amazon basin

decorPreferences: room for emersed growth above the waterline (optional)

summary: Parrot's Feather is a fast-growing stem plant from the Amazon basin, known for its whorled, feathery foliage that often emerges above the waterline. Cold-tolerant and pond-suitable, it is legally restricted or banned outright in many regions due to its invasive spread.
careNotes:
Parrot's Feather (Myriophyllum aquaticum) is a perennial stem plant native to the Amazon River basin of South America. It produces whorls of 4–6 finely divided, feathery leaves at each node. Submerged foliage is typically bright chartreuse-green, while stems and leaves that grow above the waterline turn a darker blue-green and become more pronouncedly feathery, often rising 15–30 cm above the surface. The plant grows readily both submerged and emersed, reaching a submerged height of roughly 15–40 cm, with emergent stems capable of much greater length in open ponds. Water parameters of around 18–28 °C and pH 6.0–7.5 (tolerant up to roughly 8.0) are broadly accepted, with moderate to moderately hard water (gH around 5–16). Growth rate is generally reported as fast under good conditions. The plant is unusually cold-tolerant for an aquarium species and can be overwintered outdoors in many temperate ponds, making it a practical choice for outdoor water features in mild climates.

INVASIVE STATUS: This species is widely regarded as invasive and is legally restricted in many jurisdictions. In the United Kingdom it is listed on Schedule 9 of the Wildlife & Countryside Act and is banned from sale; the EU has listed it as an invasive alien species of Union concern since 2016. Australia and New Zealand also list it as a noxious aquatic weed. Hobbyists must check local regulations before acquiring this plant, and trimmings should be disposed of by drying or composting — never released into waterways. Outside South America, all known populations are female and reproduce exclusively via stem and rhizome fragments, which is precisely what makes escape into the wild so easy. Stems are somewhat brittle, so handle them gently when planting and trimming, and avoid keeping this plant with substrate-digging fish.
breedingNotes: Parrot's Feather is propagated vegetatively. Terminal stem cuttings of 5–15 cm are the most common method: the lowest whorl of leaves is removed and the cutting is replanted gently into nutrient-rich substrate, where it roots readily. Lateral side-shoots and rhizome fragments also root without difficulty. Outside its native South American range, all known plants are female and reproduction is entirely vegetative — which is precisely why discarded fragments establish so easily in the wild and underpin the species' invasive status. Trimmings should always be disposed of by drying or composting, never by releasing them into waterways.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied Tropica search URL did not surface a Tropica species page for M. aquaticum (Tropica catalogues M. mattogrossense and M. sp. 'Guyana' but not M. aquaticum), so Buce Plant — the manifest's designated secondary — was promoted to primary. Buce Plant lists the species at moderate-to-high light, CO2 recommended for best growth, pH 6.0–7.5, mature height 4–16 in (10–40 cm), and provides the invasive-species shipping-restriction notice (cannot ship to IL, IN, MI, MN, NY, OH, PA, WA, WI).

additional.name: aqua-fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–28 °C optimum, tolerant 18–28 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), water hardness (8–16 dGH), bright lighting requirement, optional CO2, fast growth, submerged height 15–30 cm, and family Haloragaceae. Rates difficulty as easy-to-moderately-difficult; combined with the invasive-species concern this entry uses careLevel = intermediate.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Haloragaceae, order Saxifragales), native range (South America / Amazon basin), morphology (whorls of 4–6 feathery leaves; emersed leaves resemble small fir trees), and invasive status (banned in UK/EU since 2016; US bans in AL, CT, MA, ME, VT, WA). Confirms reproduction outside South America is asexual via fragments and all plants are female.

additional.name: Royal Horticultural Society
additional.notes: Cross-checked invasive listing (Schedule 9 of the UK Wildlife & Countryside Act, banned from sale) and cold-tolerance (hardiness rating H4, hardy through most of the UK without winter protection) — supporting the pond-suitable, cold-tolerant note in careNotes.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Listed as an invasive aquatic weed in many jurisdictions: banned from sale in the UK (Schedule 9 of the Wildlife & Countryside Act) and EU (invasive alien species of Union concern since 2016); sale or shipping restricted in multiple US states (including IL, IN, MI, MN, NY, OH, PA, WA, WI, AL, CT, MA, ME, VT) and listed as a noxious weed in Australia and New Zealand. Hobbyists must verify local regulations before acquiring this plant and never dispose of trimmings into waterways.
